data.title: Organigram Global Reports Record Q2 2025 Revenues As Global Footprint Expands
data.description:
Organigram Global (NASDAQ:OGI) recently reported improved financial performance for Q2-2025. The company showed record revenues for the quarter, as well as net income. The record performance was due to the company’s acquisition of Motif. The company’s stock price is down 32% over the last twelve months. It is down 4% since I covered it last February on Seeking Alpha. The company is expanding its grow and production facilities to meet international demand. With higher revenues and firm expansion plans, the company has several synergies for future revenue growth. I continue my rating of a Hold.

Organigram Global Q2 2025 Earnings Results

Organigram Global reported revenues of CA$65.5 million, a 74% increase YoY and 84% increase QoQ. The increase is due to the inclusion of revenues from Motif and from international sales. The amount is a record high for the company and possibly a new standard per quarter. International sales were CA$6.1 million, representing a 177% increase YoY.

Gross profit was CA$18.4 million, representing an increase of 91.6% YoY and an increase of 33% QoQ. Profit margin was 28% compared to 25.5% YoY and 32% QoQ. Profit margin was a bit lower this quarter due to Motif performance. The company reported a net income of CA$42.5 million versus a net loss of CA$27.1 million YoY. The amount marked a significant improvement YoY and QoQ. The net income was partially attributed to the increase in value of certain derivatives and holdings.

The company reported CA$41.5 million in proceeds from the BAT fund (Jupiter pool). Organigram still has CA$58 million remaining in the fund for strategic investments. So far, the company has used the fund to acquire Open Book Extracts in the US and Sanity Group in Germany.

Organigram Global reported CA$26 million in operating expenses, which represents a historical high for the company. Expansion of the company’s facilities and company acquisitions have driven up operating expenses higher. The company also absorbed the operating expenses of Motif during Q2.

The company reported CA$83.4 million in cash and short-term investments. It reports negligible debt. Liquidity has decreased for the company due to acquisitions and CapEx. Assets (CA$537.9) still outweigh liabilities (CA$112.3). Accounts receivables have increased YoY and QoQ.

Market consensus for Organigram Global’s next earnings release in August is CA$67.88 million, representing an increase YoY and QoQ. The company did not give a specific outlook for the next quarter, but it reports a high likelihood of elevated revenues for the rest of the year.

Current Synergies

Organigram Global still holds the number one market share in Canada, including vapes, pre-rolls, milled flowers, hash, and CBD gummies. It ranks third place in edibles and dried flowers. The acquisition of Motif boosted the company’s market share.

The company has a large international footprint with operations in the UK, Germany, Australia, and the US. The company recently acquired the Collective Project Limited. Organigram Global through this company will manufacture and sell cannabis drinks in Canada and hemp-derived THC cannabis drinks in ten US states. Organigram Global will expand its domestic BOXHOT vape brand in Australia.

Organigram Global has been vamping up and expanding its grow and production facilities. It moved all THC extraction to its Aylmer facility, where it plans to increase production. Its Moncton facility has been upgraded with LEDs and expanded to meet international demand. The company’s Winnipeg facility is being converted into a beverage manufacturing and other edibles. The company also updated its London warehouse/distribution facility for better efficiency. More details about the company’s current synergies may be found in its earnings call transcript and additional information in its earnings press release.

Overall, the company has expanded its geographic and operational footprint with entry into the US hemp-derived THC markets and Canadian cannabis beverage markets. The company is expanding its production capabilities for higher international distribution. These developments should indicate higher revenues going forward. They will also come at higher operating costs.

Organigram Global has increased its revenues over the last eight quarters. This quarter marked a historic high for the company. The increase is driven by new acquisitions and higher sales overall. Gross profit is also historically high. With these historic results come higher operating costs and revenue costs. It is hoped that net income will become more common and that the company will sooner or later report free cash flow.

The company’s stock price remains undervalued. It trades below its book value and NAV per share value. Its forward multipliers, like total EV / Revenues, also indicate undervaluation. The company has shown improved financial performance, but the cannabis sector remains weak, and investor sentiment is uncertain.

Investment Strategy and Risk

Organigram Global is at low risk of poor financial performance. The company has a growing international footprint and holds the top market share in Canada. There is some risk with the company’s cash and short-term holdings decreasing. At the same time, the company has increased its revenue numbers and still has money to draw from the Jupiter pool. There is some risk in the company’s US hemp-derived cannabis operations, since regulation in this area is still uncertain.

A Hold rating for the company comes at a low to moderate risk to the investor. Since US regulation of cannabis remains a mystery, the cannabis sector is weak. The stocks in this sector are down, and good financial performance does not guarantee a higher stock price. Cannabis stocks have undergone volatility, and Organigram Global’s stock will likely remain undervalued.

Cannabis stocks are down across the board and prices remain undervalued. Cronos, Aurora Cannabis, and Organigram Global have all reported net income, while the rest operate at a net loss. Cronos now holds the most liquidity of the group. Tilray is about to undergo a reverse split, while Village Farms recently broke the $1 per share mark. All of the Canadian cannabis LPs have international cannabis sales and operations, except for SNDL. All of the companies except for Aurora Cannabis have current or future operations in the US cannabis and hemp markets. SNDL and Tilray sell alcohol, while Village Farms sells fruits and Aurora Cannabis sells wholesale plants. For now, Cronos, Aurora Cannabis, and Organigram are leading the pack. Each company has its own strategy for growth and expansion.

Conclusion

Organigram Global is reporting increasing revenues and improved financial performance. The company has increased its footprint and brought in new revenue streams. The company is updating its growing and production facilities to handle its international cannabis distribution as well as domestic demand. The company boasts top market share in Canada. Future earnings will include revenue from Collective Projects. I rate the company as a Hold for now due to sector uncertainty.

data.title: Curbline: Rapidly Growing Into Its Valuation
data.description:
Curbline Properties (NYSE:CURB) is a high-end shopping center REIT focused on small shop retail in high household income areas. It was spun off from Site Centers (SITC) in October 2024. With a couple of quarters under its belt as an independent company, we are adding it to our coverage universe at Portfolio Income Solutions.

This article will discuss CURB’s:

  • Property portfolio
  • Leasing performance
  • Growth prospects
  • Valuation
  • Risks
  • Our overall take on the stock

Let’s get to it.

Property Portfolio

Curbline owns 107 properties with about 3.4 million leasable square feet. Its top markets are Atlanta, Phoenix, Miami, Houston, and Charlotte.

We believe these are generally good markets for shopping centers, as these MSAs have strong job and population growth.

CURB’s properties are toward the higher end of the spectrum, with significantly above-average rent per foot at $35.14. This metric may slightly overstate the luxury factor, as CURB is predominantly small shop space with minimal anchor store exposure.

Anchor tenants in big boxes tend to pay much lower rent per foot, which pulls down the rent per foot metrics of peers that tend to have substantially more big box exposure.

Even still, I view CURB’s properties as sitting toward the higher end.

The reduced big-box exposure is a desirable factor in the current state of retail, as these are often the more difficult spaces to lease. Small shops also have more landlords favoring economics.

Leasing performance

So far, the leasing numbers are good.

In the recently reported Q1'25, CURB saw +14.2% blended cash renewal spreads and +26.9% blended GAAP spreads.

With just a couple of quarters as a stand-alone, there is not a long track record of leasing, but these numbers are similar to what we have seen at many shopping center REITs, so I suspect they are sustainable and normal for CURB.

Notably, leased occupancy is 250 basis points higher than commenced occupancy (96% versus 93.5%), which implies there is significant contractually baked-in growth from the SNO leases (signed, not occupied).

96% occupancy is essentially full for shopping centers, so I would not anticipate much occupancy upside from here. Instead, growth will come from rental rate increases and property acquisitions.

Growth outlook

CURB is likely to have rapid AFFO/share growth from a combination of moderate organic growth and rapid external growth from acquisitions.

Its organic growth is positioned to be healthy and in line with peer shopping center REITs. Leasing spreads, combined with the following lease expiration schedule, will provide a few points of same-store NOI growth for the next decade.

With roughly 10% of leases rolling each year and approximately 30% mark-to-market, same-store NOI should be about 3% annually.

That is a nice baseline for CURB as well as shopping center peers.

The more unique aspect of CURB’s growth is its enormous cash position. As part of the spinoff, CURB was funded with $800 million in cash and no debt. Some of that has already been spent on acquisitions, but as of 3/31/25, about $594 million remains. Netting out a $100 million term loan, CURB has a net cash position of about $494 million.

This allows them to acquire assets very accretive while still remaining low debt. CURB can acquire about $1B of shopping centers with the cash, 50/50 funded with debt.

David Lukes, Curbline’s CEO, indicated 6.25% cap rates on the Q1'25 earnings call.

“In terms of cap rates, we're still blending to around 6.25%. If you look at the past trailing couple of quarters. That can get pretty lumpy. When you get into the individual assets, it can be in the mid-5s, and it can be upwards of 7% depending on whether there's a vacancy and what the lease duration looks like on the rent roll, the amount of credit. But in general, we're still blending to a low 6%.”

In Q1'25, CURB had $5.65 million in interest income, which implies their cash is earning about 3.8%. As cash is spent acquiring properties, interest income will be lost. Thus, the net accretion of property acquisitions will be roughly:

(Acquisition volume) X (cap rate – WACC)

  • Cost of debt is about 5%
  • Cost of cash from lost interest is about 3.8%
  • So blended WACC is about 4.4%

With acquisition cap rates averaging 6.25%, CURB has a spread of 1.85%. On $1B of acquisitions (using cash on hand mixed 50/50 with debt) that is net AFFO accretion of $18.5 million of about 18 cents per share.

Current guidance is $1.005 at the midpoint, so that is fully 18% growth in the process of putting their cash to work.

Curbline Valuation

Curbline appears expensive at first glance, with an AFFO multiple of about 23X.

However, adjustments do need to be made given the highly unusual capital structure with such a large cash position. As such, I would lean toward valuing CURB on enterprise value rather than multiple.

With an annualized NOI of $113.9 million, we can value CURB’s properties using the following cap rate table. The implied market cap is larger than the property value because of the net cash position.

The current market price is valued at CURB at a cap rate of just under 6%.

Further adjustments can be made for mark-to-market of CURB’s leases, which are substantially below market rate.

To begin, we note that CURB’s NOI is almost identical to its ABR, which is $111 million.

This is because most retail leases are triple net, so CURB gets reimbursed by tenants for most of the Opex.

Further, we know the price per foot at which leases are being signed. In Q1'25, CURB’s ABR per foot on new and renewal leases was $41.15, which is significantly above the $35.14 of its existing leases.

However, getting leases to market rates is not free, as it involves tenant improvement and leasing commission costs. So the number that best represents the stabilized NOI of the portfolio is going to be the net effective rent highlighted in yellow below.

If we use this number, it implies CURB’s stabilized NOI of its existing portfolio is about $128 million annually, which produces the following valuation table.

At $23.15, CURB is trading at a cap rate in the high 6s on mark-to-market NOI.

In my opinion, that is slightly undervalued as CURB’s properties are closer to low 6s stabilized cap rate based on location and other metrics.

In my opinion, the fair value of CURB is around $25.

Risks to CURB

In addition to the standard risks facing its sector, CURB has idiosyncratic risks of its cash pile.

Much of CURB’s success hinges on how well it can get that capital deployed. We will be watching to see how cap rates move and the velocity of CURB’s acquisitions. Some of it depends on management skill, but there is also a significant portion that is out of their control. The ideal environment for CURB would be property cap rates rising in the near term, with a large volume of properties up for sale. The higher the cap rates available, the more accretive the deployment of its cash will be. It is plausible, however, that rapid movements in interest rates or geopolitical events could freeze property acquisitions, thereby delaying accretion.

CURB is cashflow positive and stable, so they do not need to rush. It would simply be a greater benefit to AFFO if they can get acquisition cashflowing sooner rather than later.

Overall take

We believe CURB is undervalued, with a fair value about 10% above the current market price.

Worth noting is that other shopping center REITs appear to be more discounted, with many in the sector trading 20%-30% below fair value. As such, CURB likely has less upside than certain peers.

That said, I can also see CURB having better market price movement in the near term, just because it will appear to be outperforming due to the rapid AFFO/share growth. AFFO growth from deployment of the cash hoard is one-time in nature, but it might be easier for the market to pick up on as compared to the more nuanced 10-year growth horizons of peers predicated on mark-to-market and SNO kick-in.

Overall, I think CURB is a viable investment positioned to deliver a slightly above-market return. Fundamentally speaking, I would rather own the deeper value in the sector, but there is also potential to trade CURB well on its AFFO growth momentum.

data.title: Red Cat: Patience Warranted
data.description:
Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:RCAT) entered the last quarter with the dilemma of limited growth targets versus the forecasts for the full. The drone company has every sign strong business is lined up for the 2H and into 2026, but the stock is now languishing at the lows. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish on the stock here with investors required to be patient for sales to reach targets.

Immaterial Quarter

Red Cat came into the quarter with limited sales expectations in comparison to the massive drone opportunity making the Q1 '25 financials virtually worthless.

The company didn't fail to disappoint with actual revenue of only $1.6 million. Red Cat did clear up the opportunity more succinctly.

On the Q1 '25 earnings call, CEO Jeff Thompson highlighted the backlog starts generating meaningful sales in June as follows (emphasis added):

First, I'm excited to share the Black Widow manufacturing started this month to begin deliveries to the Army in June. We currently have a non-SRR backlog of $10 million for Black Widow and Edge-130s to start delivering in June. As far as our guidance goes, we are reiterating our 2025 annual revenue guidance of $80 million to $120 million. Also, please note, consistent with previous communications, we also reiterate that the bulk of this revenue will be recognized in the latter half of 2025.

Red Cat reiterated 2025 annual revenue guidance of $80 to $120 million for calendar year 2025, which consists of the following:

  • $25 to $65 million in SRR-related Black Widow sales
  • $25 million in Non-SRR Black Widow sales
  • $25 million in Edge 130 sales
  • $5 million in Fang FPV sales.

The market doesn't appear to realize the revenue target for the last 3 quarters of the year is basically $78 to $118 million after Red Cat only reported $2 million of sales in Q1. Even the Q2 sales target is currently only at $7 million, based partly on the above statement of the $10 million backlog for drone deliveries starting in June.

The company would have $70+ million in sales and upwards of $110 million left for the 2H. At the high end, Red Cat could actually reach a $50 million quarter after only producing less than $2 million in Q1'25.

Back on the earnings call, CEO Jeff Thompson went further to comment on the sales potential of the Edge-130 drone as follows:

We continue to have very strong demand for the Edge-130. Shawn Webb, our new President, is doing a great job scaling. But to get to that 150 drones per month by the end of the year, we'll run out of space. 150 drones per month is approximately $81 million in annual run rate. So instead of moving in 6 months, we are ripping off the band-aid now and moving into a larger facility, same landlords with no penalties or paying two rents.

The revenue metric provided by the CEO amounts to $45K per Edge-130 drone and revenues of $6.75 million per month on production of 150 drones, or ~$20 million per quarter. The Edge-130 drone isn't even the high sales drone.

As mentioned on the call, Red Cat wasn't building the Black Widow drones until getting the final version of the drone requirements from their biggest customer (U.S. Army Short Range Reconnaissance Program) until March 27th. The company is busy expanding capacity to meet the large demand as the U.S. military quickly looks to move towards unmanned military equipment.

Not Price For Huge Sale Ramp

Red Cat only has a market cap of around $600 million and the consensus analyst estimates have revenues jumping from next to nothing now to $80 million this year. The estimates call for another big jump to $150 million next year, which appears conservative considering revenues could approach $50 million in Q4 alone and consensus estimates are at $41 million now based on the low end of guidance for 2025.

The company just raised $30 million in cash to push the cash balance to $39 million. In addition, Red Cat already has $17 million in inventory to provide the assets to get the business off the ground.

The stock has the market cap to make the Russell 2000 reconstitution announced next Friday. The addition into a major stock index will provide Red Cat with more visibility for a stock trading at ~4x sales targets for 2026 while growth is forecast to hit nearly 100%.

The biggest risk to the stock story is a manufacturing glitch. Red Cat is trying to scale multiple drones at different location to material sales and any major disruption could impact the ability to fulfill contracts and maintain military customers.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Red Cat is cheap compared to the massive opportunity in drones and unmanned vessels. Investors should use the stock weakness to load up on Red Cat right before a major ramp occurs providing a huge catalyst for the stock.

data.title: Replimune Group: Analysts Are More Optimistic Than The Market
data.description:
Shares of oncolytic immunotherapy concern Replimune Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:REPL) plunged 28% in two trading sessions (May 6-7, 2025) on big volume with no company or advanced melanoma news. Its lead therapy (RP1) in combination with anti-PD-1 therapy, Opdivo, has a July 22, 2025, PDUFA date as a second-line (2L) therapy for the treatment of anti-PD-1 failed advanced melanoma. With solid data backing its application but lingering reservations concerning the oncolytic virus therapy approach, the recent unexplained drop in this clinical stage biotech concern merited further investigation. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

Replimune Group, Inc. is a Woburn, Massachusetts-based clinical-stage biotechnology concern focused on the development of oncolytic immunotherapies to improve the lives of cancer patients. Oncolytic immunotherapy involves directing certain viruses to selectively replicate in and directly kill cancer cells while triggering an antitumor immune response. The company has two active clinical programs led by RP1, for which it is awaiting a decision from the FDA by July 22, 2025, for the treatment of anti-PD-1 failed advanced melanoma. Replimune was founded in 2015 and went public in 2018, raising net proceeds of $103.3 million at $15 a share. Its stock trades around $7.25 a share with an approximate $560 million market cap.

Immunotherapy Landscape

The current immunotherapy paradigm revolves around checkpoint inhibition, where a drug made of antibodies blocks ‘normal’ proteins on cancer cells or proteins on T cells, allowing the T cells to correctly identify a cancer cell, at which point it unleashes an immune response. This approach works only if the T cells are already engaged with the cancer cells (i.e., they are ‘hot’). Since most cancer patients do not have an ongoing immune response to their respective tumors, the effectiveness of checkpoint inhibition can be constrained. Replimune aims to render all solid tumor types (hot and cold) susceptible to checkpoint inhibitors by introducing an engineered and armed strain of herpes simplex virus 1 (HSV-1) into the tumor.

Oncolytic Therapy

Following direct injection into tumors, Replimune’s viruses replicate in cancer cells and then lyse (decompose) them, releasing tumor antigens, including neo-antigens specific to the patient, which could otherwise be hidden from the immune system. This process triggers various pathways of the innate immune system, all of which release interferon that activates natural killer cells to destroy cancer cells. On the adaptive side of immunity, antigen-presenting cells, which are attracted to the now-injected tumor, corral cancer antigens and present them to the body’s T cells, which are then charged with cancer cell destruction; thus transforming the cancer cells from cold to hot, leaving them vulnerable to checkpoint inhibition. Additionally, Replimune’s assets are encoded with therapeutic genes designed to aid in the tumor destruction process. These ‘off-the-shelf’ therapies are relatively inexpensive to manufacture and easy to deliver.

That said, this approach has not achieved any meaningful commercial success. The only approved oncolytic HSV-1 virus therapy is Amgen’s (AMGN) Imlygic (talimogene laherparepvec), commonly referred to as T-VEC, which was approved by the FDA for the treatment of metastatic melanoma in 2015. It demonstrated a 16.9% complete response (CR) rate in a Phase 3 trial and has shown CR rates up to 61.5% as a monotherapy in real-world usage. It also exhibited synergies with Bristol Myers Squibb’s (BMY) anti-CTLA-4 monoclonal antibody Yervoy (ipilimumab), more than doubling the response rate in the clinic. Despite these ‘achievements’, T-VEC has not demonstrated improvement in overall survival and is a footnote for Amgen, not amongst its top 25 selling therapies in 2024. AstraZeneca (AZN), Boehringer Ingelheim, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Merck (MRK), Vyriad, Oncorus (OTC:ONCR), and Turnstone Biologics were pursuing oncolytic viral immunotherapies in 2021, which have either been discontinued, deprioritized, or stuck in neutral. Furthermore, even though it is alleged to work by replicating inside cancer cells, causing them to rupture and die, T-VEC’s exact mechanism of action is unknown, casting serious doubt on the entire approach.

Management at Replimune, which was responsible for the development of T-VEC at BioVex – later acquired by Amgen in 2011 – is hoping to score another oncolytic virus approval. To that end, they have two active off-the-shelf clinical programs.

Pipeline

RP1. Replimune’s most advanced candidate is RP1 (vusolimogene oderparepvec), a selectively replicating HSV-1 that expresses a highly fusogenic form of an envelope glycoprotein known as GALV-GP R(-) – which increases CD8+ T cell infiltration and PD-L1 expression levels in tumors – and another known as GM-CSF, which drives macrophage activity. In keeping with the oncolytic virus approach, RP1 is designed to induce both direct tumor-cell destruction and immune response. Replimune has filed a BLA for RP1 in combination with Bristol Myers Squibb’s (BMY) Opdivo (nivolumab) for the treatment of advanced melanoma in patients who have previously failed on an anti-PD1-containing regimen and have received an accelerated PDUFA date of July 22, 2025. The company is leaning into data from its Phase 2 trial (IGNYTE) in which the combo demonstrated a 33.6% overall response rate (ORR) with 85% of responses lasting more than 12 months and a median duration of response (mDOR) of 21.6 months. Median overall survival has not been reached, with 54.8% alive after 36 months.

These data compare favorably with response rates of ~7% for anti-PD-1 monotherapies in a 2L setting after failing in 1L. Approximately 8,000 Americans die annually from metastatic melanoma, owing to the fact that ~50% of all metastatic melanoma patients advance on initial anti-PD-1 therapy. As such, individuals with stage 4 melanoma have a five-year survival rate of 27 percent, according to the American Cancer Society.

It should be noted that Iovance’s (IOVA) tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy Amtagvi (lifileucel) is approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma (a.k.a. advanced melanoma) following prior treatment with a PD-1 inhibitor and a BRAF inhibitor if the tumor carries a BRAF V600 mutation. Approval was attained with an ORR of 31.5%. Unlike, RP1, which is off the shelf, Amtagvi is individualized and requires a patient's tumor fragment, taking 22 days to develop and administer. It has been plagued with manufacturing and launch issues but is poised to deliver FY25 revenue of ~$250 million. Additionally, privately held Obsidian Therapeutics will be presenting Phase 1 findings from its own TIL therapy (OBX-115) for the same indication at the 2025 ASCO Annual Meeting on June 2, 2025.

Replimune has initiated a 400-patient Phase 3 confirmatory trial (IGNYTE-3) that will assess the combination against physician’s choice with the primary endpoint OS.

The RP1-nivolumab pairing is also undergoing evaluation for failed anti-PD-1 non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC), which is a cohort in IGNYTE that continues to enroll patients. An update in December 2023 showed that the combination generated an ORR of 29% (12/41) at six months. With 4,600 cases of anti-PD-1 failed NMSC in the U.S. annually and no FDA approved therapies for this setting, the label expansion potential (assuming approval for advanced melanoma) is decent.

RP1 is also being assessed as a monotherapy in a Phase 1b/2 trial (ARTACUS) for the treatment of solid organ transplant NMSC, with a 35% ORR and a complete response rate (CRR) of 22% across 23 evaluable patients to date. With a target enrollment of up to 65 patients, the study is ongoing.

Lastly, RP1 was (and technically still is) undergoing investigation in combination with Regeneron’s (REGN) anti-PD-1 therapy Libtayo (cemiplimab) for the treatment of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (CSCC). The potentially registrational Phase 2 study (CERPASS) busted when the combo failed to separate from cemiplimab monotherapy in a statistically significant manner, with a CRR of 38.1% (combo) versus 25.0% (monotherapy) (p=0.040) and an ORR 52.1% (combo) versus 51.4% (monotherapy) (p=0.692). Although the trial continues to assess DOR, progression free survival (PFS), and OS, there is little hope of advancement, with shares of REPL plunging 45% to $6.75 in December 2023 when the data were released.

RP2. Replimune’s second therapy (RP2) builds on RP1. In addition to the GALV-GP R- and GM-CSF presentation, RP2 is engineered to express an antibody-like molecule intended to block the activity of CTLA-4. Its best shot of approval is in combination with nivolumab for the treatment of metastatic uveal (ocular) melanoma (mUM). In a 17-patient cohort as part of a Phase 1 study, the combo delivered an ORR of 29.4%, a disease control rate of 58.8%, and an mDOR of 11.5 months. It is now undergoing evaluation in a 280-patient pivotal Phase 2/3 trial in which patients will be randomized 1:1 to receive either the combo or nivolumab plus Bristol Myers Squibb’s Yervoy (ipilimumab) with the primary endpoints PFS (Phase 2) and mOS (Phase 3). The size of the trial seems a bit large considering there are only 1,700 new cases in the U.S. per annum. As a guidepost, Immunocore Holdings’ (IMCR) once-weekly infused bispecific targeting HLA-A*02:01 gp100 antigen Kimmtrak was approved in January 2022 after demonstrating an mOS of nearly 22 months in its registrational study.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

To help fund these studies, Replimune conducted private and public offerings in 2024, raising net proceeds of $252.5 million, providing it with unrestricted cash and short-term investments of $536.5 million against no debt as of December 31, 2024 for an operating runway into late 2026.

With an approval seemingly imminent, Street analysts are unanimously positive on the company’s prospects, featuring three buys and five outperform ratings. That said, they have been unanimously positive for at least four years. Their median twelve-month price target is right at $20 a share.

Verdict:

Shares of REPL fell 28% over two trading sessions on May 6-7, 2025 on heavy volume but on no news from either the company, competitors in the advanced melanoma space, or the sell-side analyst community. Although the Replimune’s stock has bounced back some, it is still down 21% since May 5th. The selloff seems unwarranted, given RP1 in combination with Opdivo seems poised to breakthrough in a 2L setting for advance melanoma. The question becomes one of market opportunity versus market value. Replimune estimates its domestic market opportunity with RP1 in 2L advanced melanoma at 10,500 patients per annum, but it will still face physician skepticism regarding the oncolytic virus approach.

The good news is that Iovance has completely stumbled out of the gate with its launch of Amtagvi and is only expecting to treat 110 patients in 2Q24, leaving plenty of patients for Replimune to poach. With the obvious caveat that a rejection from the FDA would be ruinous, there is room for the company to carve out a meaningful portion of the 2L advanced melanoma market, making its $25 million-premium-to-cash valuation after the recent selloff merit a small position for aggressive investors within a well-diversified biotech portfolio.

data.title: FTAI Aviation: Strategic Transition To A Modular, Margin-Focused Model
data.description:
FTAI Aviation Ltd. (NASDAQ:FTAI) is an interesting player in the aviation leasing and care market, which has transformed its business model in the last few years. Initially founded as part of the investment fund Fortress, the company decided to step into high-value niches: not only to lease planes and engines, but also to repair, modularize, and sell as used technical parts. Such a "hybrid" strategy, where one engine can be rented, then taken back, dismantled, upgraded, updated, and resold, forms a strong competitive advantage that allows for generating multiple income flows.

In 2022-2023, it was separated from the former Fortress Transportation & Infrastructure and focused solely on the aviation segment. The company operates probably the largest independent CFM56 series fleet of over 450 units, as well as V2500 engines for Airbus A320 family aircraft. The inventory of this scale is unique: it allows a reliable supply of used parts and modules to the global airlines, which are currently not always offered by manufacturers of OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer). FTAI's partnership with AAR Corp guarantees the distribution of those used parts, which is crucial when airlines seek to reduce the cost of operating and at the same time ensure safety and reliability.

However, the biggest changes occurred in the MRO (Maintenance, Repair & Overhaul). By the end of 2024, the company will operate with two major repair centers - Quickturn Miami and the newly acquired LMCES Montreal. In 2025, FTAI ​​turned to Europe, and along with the IAG Engine Center, opened a joint venture in Rome, expanding its annual engine renewal modules up to more than 1,350 units. This network allows ​​it to not only renew their engines, but also to serve third-party customers, generating larger EBITDA margins than just leasing.

Financials

We had recent Q1 2025 results, and the company achieved the best quarterly results in the last two years. Revenue jumped to $502 million compared to $331 million a year ago in the same period, more than 51% growth, this comes mainly from both the strong demand for the Aerospace Products segment and the Module Factory business development.

Even more impressive is the change in gross profit margins: it was as high as 50.5%, higher than the first quarter of 2024, which was 32.9%. Such a margin leap shows that the company not only raises revenue, but also does it effectively, being able to maintain high profitability despite the growing operating scale and growing investments.

Operating Income is $158.3 million, and the margins improved significantly. This result shows that the company can control costs and maintain price pressure in the airplane and repair market.

EPS was $0.87, almost three times more than in the same period in 2024 ($0.31). Importantly, the number of shares has remained stable (103 million), so I can say that profit growth is sustainable and undiluted. The overall Q1 results show not only growth but also business maturity - a profitable, effective, and investor oriented model that justifies the value of the company.

Quarterly growth:

Q1 2025 Q4 2024 QoQ Change Q1 2024 YoY Change
Revenue 502.1 498.8 0.70% 326.7 53.70%
Gross Profit 220.9 206.5 7.00% 158.6 39.30%
Operating Income 158.3 148.3 6.80% 100.1 58.10%
Diluted EPS 0.87 0.83 4.80% 0.31 180.60%
EBITDA 221 207.1 6.70% 154

43.50%

Catalysts

The most important short-term catalyst is the further implementation of the Strategic Capital Initiative (SCI). By March 31, 2025, 98 aircraft have already been transferred or reserved, but the overall plan is to go up to 250. Each additional transaction not only provides cash to cover debt, but also strengthens the Asset-Light business model by reducing balance sheet risks.

Second, the full start of the Quickturn Europe MRO Center (Rome) will allow for an increase in module service volume to 450 units per year in Europe. This will increase the amount of income from high-margin services and shorten service time for European customers.

Third, the start of the PMA manufacturing business. These parts, the cheaper OEM alternative, can open up new markets for smaller airlines, for which the price is the main thing when buying parts. This would be the supply of expanding products and diversifying income.

Valuation

At first glance, it may appear that the company is being traded at a significant premium when valuing ​​according to similar companies. If we look at EV/EBITDA (TTM), FTAI ​​is currently traded at 18.34x multiplier, when the sector average among the closest competitors (BZLFY, CNM, AIT, WCC) is about 12.7x. This means that the company is valued at about 44% more than the sector average. However, it is worth noting that the FTA EBITDA margin is over 30%, and in some quarters it has exceeded 40%, while most companies generate about 10-15%.

According to P/E (FY2), the FTA multiplier is 17.3x, while the sector average (without FTA) is about 14.6x. The difference here is about +18.5 %, and the market is already starting to price in the future growth of EPS, which is sustainable and based on growing demand for modular engines and Quickturn services. I do believe that the market already priced in the possible growth, however, there is still room to get a 20-30% gain here.

Risks

I have decided to do some econometrics and see if CPI, FED rates, or Consumer Confidence have a role here. According to my analysis, FTAI ​​stock price fluctuations (risk) have a clear connection to the economic situation. And here's what's interesting - the risk increases when the economy is strong. Surprisingly, FTAI’s stock volatility spikes when the economy is strong. To test this, I ran GARCH(1,1) on 10 years of monthly returns to extract a monthly volatility series, then ran OLS regressions against CPI, Fed Funds, and Consumer Confidence.

  • When inflation falls, the FTAI ​​starts to fluctuate more. If inflation decreases by 1 point, the price may range from ~ 1% more than usual.
  • When the FED reduces interest rates, fluctuations can jump to 30% higher than usual. This means that if the price usually moves ± 10% per month, it can jump ± 13% or more after interest reduction.
  • As consumer confidence grows, the company also begins to behave more volatile - more purchases, more emotions, more fluctuations.

In short, although the FTAI returns are not directly dependent on macroeconomics, its risk is. This means that the company becomes more risky exactly when optimism is in the market, and this is important to know when managing the risk of the portfolio during periods of reduced interest rates or improving economic mood.

When assessing the risks of the balance sheet, the FTAI still has a considerable debt as of 2025. Initially, the net debt was about $3.64 billion, which is not surprising for an aviation company. Interest costs remained high due to the debt. However, the SCI strategy that moves planes to the fund is expected to reduce debt to 3.0-3.5x EBITDA by the end of the year.

Summary

This is a unique player in the aviation sector that has successfully combined the traditional aircraft and motor leasing with maintenance and used parts business. Over the past year, the company has implemented a significant transformation by creating Module Factory and Quickturn programs that generate income and have high margins.

Q1 2025 results show that this strategy works: EBITDA grew by more than 60%, and the Aerospace Products segment has become a major source of profit. Dividend payouts, an increasing number of customers, and geographical development confirm long-term stability.

Ahead, the development of the SCI transactions, the acceleration of the European MRO Center, the beginning of the production of PMA parts, and the dividend policy consist of a set of strong catalysts. Although there are cyclical and interest rate risks, the company ​​has a clear vision, a strong business model, and proven ability to grow. I am rating the company as a BUY.

data.title: 2 Infrastructure-Focused CEFs Delivering Monthly Distributions
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist.

Infrastructure keeps the world moving, whether through moving or producing energy, tollways, airports, and more; these are essential to everyday life for most of civilization. Today, I'm looking at two closed-end funds that provide diversified exposure to investing in infrastructure.

Both of the funds we'll be looking at today also invest with a global sleeve in their portfolio, helping to provide even more flexibility and diversification. At the same time, they employ two different strategies with their approach, which can make them complementary to each other, rather than competing head-to-head.

#1 Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF)

  • 1-Year Z-score: 2.01
  • Discount/Premium: 3.09%
  • Distribution Yield: 7.33%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.39%
  • Leverage: 28.54%
  • Managed Assets: $3.33 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual.

UTF's objective is

"Total return, with an emphasis on income through investment in securities issued by infrastructure companies." They define infrastructure companies as those that "typically provide the physical framework that society requires to function on a daily basis and are defined as utilities, pipelines, toll roads, airports, railroads, marine ports, telecommunications companies, and other infrastructure companies.

UTF is quite a popular CEF in the infrastructure space, thanks to its long history of being rather steady and providing a reliable distribution. The fund had cut during the Global Financial Crisis; this was through switching to a quarterly distribution policy at the same time. However, for many years now, they have switched back to the more attractive monthly distribution schedule. They've also been holding it quite steady for a number of years now.

The fund takes a leveraged approach in an attempt to enhance overall returns and help to cover the fund's distribution to investors. That's unlike the other fund we'll be discussing below, the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust (BUI), which employs an options writing strategy instead.

When the Fed was raising rates aggressively, that was a headwind to most leveraged funds as they saw their borrowing costs increase. UTF wasn't able to avoid that entirely, but a large portion of their borrowings were hedged thanks to interest rate swaps. Those last for a couple of years yet, and the Fed cut 100 basis points already late in 2024. Even further, the Fed is expected to cut a couple more times this year, which means UTF has avoided the worst of the peak rates. Of course, we aren't expected to go back down to zero rates again, so they are still anticipated to see higher borrowing costs in the future.

In general, UTF will require capital gains in order to fund its attractive distribution. Currently, that distribution rate works out to 7.33%; however, the more important figure is the NAV rate that comes in at 7.55%. UTF's NAV rate is slightly higher due to the fund trading at a slight premium to its NAV per share. That said, it is still quite a reasonable rate and isn't looking too elevated—which is a positive as it suggests that the fund's current payout is sustainable.

While the fund trades at a slight premium, which can give some investors pause, a premium isn't all that unusual for this fund over the last five years. Prior to that, the fund did experience trading at a wider-than-usual discount, at a double-digit level, in fact. Though the switch back to a monthly distribution and holding the payout steady for a number of years now has likely contributed to the fund's more regular premium trading level.

I believe that it is still a fine fund to add to through a dollar-cost average approach, even if it isn't a screaming bargain. The 1-year z-score approaching 2 is also an indication that it is more on the overvalued side in the short term.

In looking at the top holdings, we see a solid mixture of energy-related infrastructure, as well as utility companies and even American Tower (AMT) for some tower REIT exposure.

The fund is primarily invested in equity positions, but they do carry around 15% exposure to corporate bonds and another 3% to preferred securities. They listed a 57% allocation to U.S. companies, with another 11% weighted to Canada, 5% to the U.K., and 4% to Australia. The other 23% is listed simply as "other".

#2 BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust (BUI)

  • 1-Year Z-score: 1.01
  • Discount/Premium: 0.60%
  • Distribution Yield: 7%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.08%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $522.5 million
  • Structure: Perpetual.

BUI's investment objective is to "provide total return through a combination of current income, current gains, and long-term capital appreciation".

To achieve this objective, they have quite a bit of flexibility. They will invest:

"primarily in equity securities issued by companies that are engaged in the Utilities, Infrastructure, and Power Opportunities business segments anywhere in the world and by utilizing an option writing (selling) strategy in an effort to enhance current gains."

As noted above, BUI utilizes an options writing strategy rather than being leveraged through borrowings. By writing covered calls on the underlying positions in the fund, they can collect the option premiums to help cover their monthly distribution to investors. BUI has never cut its distribution, but it wasn't around prior to the GFC either. They had initially started with a quarterly distribution policy, but then switched to a monthly payout. More recently, they even increased the distribution.

A covered call writing strategy can limit some upside potential if the underlying securities are rising rapidly, where the price rises above the strike price selected. However, the management team for BUI has flexibility here. They usually overwrite their portfolio at 30-40%, so only a minor slice of their portfolio is often overwritten. As of the last reporting, it actually came in at 33.33%, to be more specific.

The distribution rate for BUI comes to 7%, with its NAV rate coming in at a similar 7.04%. Again, though, we have a scenario where the NAV rate is ever so slightly higher here, meaning we know the fund is trading at a slight premium. Similar to UTF, we can see that a premium isn't all that unusual for BUI either. In fact, the fund has an even longer history of trading right near parity with its NAV or even higher.

That once again suggests that BUI could still be a worthwhile investment to add to in smaller chunks, even if it isn't in the bargain bin.

For BUI's top holdings, we similarly see a mixture of energy infrastructure and utility names dominate the top.

For BUI, they actually listed that U.S. exposure is 66.31% of the fund's overall allocation. That puts it at a slightly higher concentration in the U.S., weighting relative to UTF. It might be interesting to note, though, that BUI holds only 53 positions. UTF notes 259 different holdings, making it significantly more diversified when it comes to underlying holdings. Yet, when it comes to top ten percentage allocation, BUI comes in at just shy of 34%—almost identical to UTF's 34.3%.

Conclusion

UTF and BUI provide exposure to global infrastructure investments. Infrastructure is critical for everyday human life to function properly and comfortably. The sorts of investments that tend to be rather boring overall, but their boring nature is exactly what leads to relatively steady and predictable cash flows. Those cash flows and capital gains are then passed through to investors through their monthly distributions. While neither is particularly a screaming deal at the time, they are still both worthwhile long-term investments that investors could allocate smaller chunks of capital toward.

data.title: OneSpan Continues Transition As Growth Remains Slow
data.description:
Investment Outlook

OneSpan (NASDAQ:OSPN) recently reported its Q1 2025 financial results, beating both revenue and earnings per share estimates.

I previously analyzed OSPN in April 2024 with a Hold outlook due to slowing topline revenue growth as the firm changed its focus toward profitability.

The company is sunsetting various aspects of its revenue streams and is seeing some decision-making delays with large prospects, and the potential for Forex headwinds remains material.

So, I remain a neutral Hold on OSPN as it continues to work through its transition to a fully SaaS focused company, and the Board weighs potential capital allocation decisions.

OneSpan’s Market And Approach

OneSpan’s primary revenue segment comes from its various Security Solutions products, which produced $182 million in revenue in 2024, while its Digital Agreements segment contributed $61 million during the year.

The company's main focus is on the banking, finance, and insurance industries, but it also sells to healthcare and government segments.

Per a 2023 MarketsAndMarkets market research report, the global eSignature market is expected to grow from $9.9 billion in 2024 to $70.2 billion by 2030.

If accomplished, this would equate to a whopping CAGR of 38.5% through 2030, so the company's focus on growing its Digital Agreement business should have strong macro growth dynamics in its favor.

The report says that software-based solutions will produce the highest growth drivers, with the BFSI (Banking, Financial Services, Insurance) industry expected to generate the strongest growth rate through 2030.

By region, the Asia-Pacific region is forecasted to grow the quickest in terms of growth rate.

However, the market features intense competition and a wide variety of competitive vendors from large to smaller players, making margins potentially difficult to sustain.

The firm derives most of its revenue from Security Solutions, which it manages for maximum profitability, but its growth prospects come from its Digital Agreements segment. It generated more revenue in 2024 from the EMEA regions than from the Americas, as the pie charts show here:

OneSpan sells its software, whether in the U.S. or internationally, via a combination of in-house efforts and through distributors, resellers, or system integrators.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT of the company's primary characteristics:

Strengths

  • Market prominence in the banking sector and highly regulated industries, diversified globally

Weaknesses

  • Small market capitalization, limited brand outside the banking sector

Opportunities

  • Digital agreement market growth prospects, integration of emerging technologies

Threats

  • Intense competition from point and horizontal players, international compliance environment, cyber threats

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has declined due to expected drops in hardware and planned sunsetting services. Operating income by quarter (red line) has improved because of a higher gross margin plateau and reduced SG&A and R&D costs against revenue growth.

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has remained solid as a result of a favorable revenue mix. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have moved lower in the most recent quarter due to cost discipline efforts. R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) have also trended lower on reduced investment, likely in its Security Solutions segment, which it is managing for profitability over growth.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have improved in recent quarters on higher gross margins and lower SG&A and R&D expenses.

(All data in the above charts is GAAP.)

For balance sheet results, OneSpan ended the quarter with $105 million in cash and equivalents and no debt.

In the past 12 months, OSPN’s stock price has risen by 25% vs. the same gain by the benchmark iShares Expanded Technology-Software ETF (IGV), as the chart indicates below.

Below is a handy major financial and operating metrics table for OneSpan that shows both forward and trailing four-quarter results and metrics:

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

The company's Rule of 40 performance is shown in the table below:

Rule of 40 Performance (Unadjusted)

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

The table indicates that topline revenue has now gone into reverse, but operating margin improvement has more than made up for it, at least in terms of this metric.

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was $50.3 million, and capital expenditures were $7.8 million.

The company paid $10.2 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was $40.1

The net dollar decrease in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $4.8 million.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 4.2%, which is somewhat high for a mature company.

Net stock buybacks versus SBC have been less than half, so the company has been diluting shareholders in favor of employees, at least in terms of net outstanding share growth.

Management’s plans for capital allocation in relation to shares are unclear, as it has said the board is ‘weighing’ potential growth in capital return to shareholders after declaring its second $0.12 quarterly dividend.

Why I’m Neutral On OneSpan

In 2023, management indicated that it would run its Digital Agreements segment focused on growth, but its Security Solutions segment for its cash flow and less focused on growth.

Since then, this has resulted in Digital Agreements growing from 22% of revenue in 2023 to 25% in Q1 2025.

OneSpan’s overall growth strategy has been to focus on subscription revenue, making selective acquisitions to that effect and increasing its international presence, which is already robust.

However, in the wake of the recent US tariff policy changes, its international business may suffer longer decision cycles as a result.

I’ve created a graphic showing the frequency of various negative keywords in the most recent conference with analysts:

The chart shows that OneSpan is experiencing some customer delays in decision-making, pushing big client wins into Q2 2025 at the earliest.

Regarding valuation, the market is valuing OSPN at a forward EV/Sales multiple of around 2.1x on an estimated NTM revenue growth rate of an anemic 3.6% against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of approximately 15% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a recent median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110% vs. OneSpan’s NRR of 107%, which is reasonably close.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 5.0x on May 5, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, OSPN is currently valued by the market at a significant discount to the broader index as of May 5, 2025, likely due to its subpar topline revenue growth potential in the near future.

Looking ahead, the company expects double-digit subscription revenue growth, but continued negative effects of sunsetting various revenue sources, slower timing of customer orders, and higher tariff costs on its hardware items.

Also, changes in the Forex could hurt its results, especially if the US dollar gains strength against other currencies.

As a result, overall growth for OneSpan could be in the low single-digits for 2025 and profitability at the same level or even slightly lower.

While I can rule out potential M&A or share buyback catalysts that could produce short-term upside for the stock, I don’t see anything that would result in a meaningful upward catalyst.

As such, my outlook for OSPN remains a neutral Hold.
